data.title: Big tech eyes $60B bet on OpenAI as Nvidia, Amazon, Microsoft enter talks: report
data.description:
• Nvidia (NVDA), Amazon (AMZN), and Microsoft (MSFT) are reportedly in talks to invest up to $60 billion ‌in OpenAI (OPENAI).
  • Nvidia (NVDA), an existing investor whose chips power OpenAI's AI models, is in talks to invest up to $30 billion, The Information reported on Wednesday.
  • OpenAI is close to receiving ⁠term sheets, or an investment commitment, from these ‍firms, the report said.
  • Amazon's (AMZN) investment could depend on separate negotiations, including a possible expansion of OpenAI's cloud ⁠server rental deal with ⁠Amazon and ​a commercial agreement for OpenAI to sell its products, such as enterprise ChatGPT subscriptions, to Amazon, The Information said.
  • This development follows reports from ‍earlier this week that said that SoftBank (SFTBY) is in talks to invest as much as an additional $30 billion in OpenAI.

data.title: C.H. Robinson targets double-digit productivity improvements in 2026 through Lean AI expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc. (CHRW) Q4 2025

Management View

  • David Bozeman, President and CEO, opened by highlighting, “The fourth quarter certainly provided a challenging macro environment with weak global freight demand, rising spot costs in trucking and falling ocean rates all providing headwinds to our business.” He emphasized that despite these pressures, “we grew our total volume by 1% and our truckload volume by approximately 3% on a year-over-year basis compared to a 7.6% year-over-year decline in the Cass Freight Shipment Index. This reflects another quarter of demonstrable market share growth.”
  • Bozeman stressed the ongoing transformation, stating, “We are using our trusted domain expertise, to build technology that delivers on our customer promise and drives higher value for all of our stakeholders.” He underscored the company’s commitment to scaling Lean AI initiatives, noting, “Significant runway exists as we continue to deepen the Lean mindset and scale custom-built AI agents across the enterprise.”
  • Damon Lee, Chief Financial Officer, stated, “Q4 personnel expenses were $337 million, including $15.2 million of restructuring charges related to workforce reductions. Excluding restructuring charges in 2025 and 2024, our Q4 personnel expenses were $321.8 million, down $28.8 million or 8.2% due to our continued productivity and cost optimization efforts and the divestiture of our Europe Surface Transportation business.” Lee added, “As a result of our efforts to grow market share, improve gross margins and increase our productivity and operating leverage, we expanded our operating margin, excluding restructuring costs, by 320 basis points year-over-year.”
  • Michael Castagnetto, President of North American Surface Transportation, detailed market share gains, saying, “For the 11th consecutive quarter, the team delivered market share growth in Q4... our combined truckload and LTL volume delivered positive growth of approximately 1% year-over-year. Truckload volume rose approximately 3% and LTL volume increased approximately 0.5% year-over-year.”
  • Arun Rajan, Chief Strategy & Innovation Officer, described the technology strategy: “We own and control the code and we own the application layer because we are building our own AI agents. We, therefore, have more control over the implementation process and the speed of integrating those agents with our proprietary technology landscape.”

Outlook

  • Lee indicated, “We expect 2026 personnel expenses to be in the guidance range of $1.25 billion to $1.35 billion. This includes an expectation that we will generate double-digit productivity improvements in both NAST and Global Forwarding in 2026 as we continue to implement agentic AI across our quote-to-cash life cycle of an order.”
  • SG&A expenses for 2026 are expected to be $540 million to $590 million, with depreciation and amortization of $95 million to $105 million for the year.
  • For 2026, the full year tax rate is forecasted to range from 18% to 20%. Capital expenditures are guided at $75 million to $85 million for the year.
  • Lee noted, “On a quarterly basis, it's important to note that our Q1 personnel expenses are expected to increase sequentially due to the employer portion of FICA taxes resetting to a higher level... This impact is estimated to be approximately $15 million in Q1 versus Q4.”

Financial Results

  • Lee reported, “Q4 total revenue in AGP declined approximately 7% and 4% year-over-year, respectively. The AGP decline was driven by a 13% year-over-year decline in Global Forwarding AGP, which was primarily due to a significant drop in ocean rates... partially offset by a 2% increase in NAST AGP.”
  • The company generated $305.4 million in cash from operations in Q4 and ended the quarter with approximately $1.49 billion of liquidity.
  • Net debt-to-EBITDA leverage at the end of Q4 was 1.03x, down from 1.17x at the end of Q3.
  • The company returned approximately $207.7 million of cash to shareholders in Q4 through $133.3 million of share repurchases and $74.3 million of dividends.

Q&A

  • Thomas Wadewitz, UBS: Asked about adjusted gross profit growth in Q1 and longer-term margin potential. Lee responded, “December was heavily impacted by global forwarding and the ocean rate normalization... we expect that to continue into Q1 as well.” Regarding margins, “We're still on a really good trajectory to get to those mid-cycle margins that we laid out for NAST and Global Forwarding with 40% being the margin target for NAST at mid-cycle.”
  • Bascome Majors, Susquehanna: Inquired about balancing margin expansion and share growth in 2026. Lee answered, “We want to make the right decision for what I consider quality earnings growth. Once we get to what we consider is our threshold for quality of earnings, which we've set at already, right? 40% for NAST at mid-cycle and 30% for Global Forwarding... we'll make the decision beyond that point on what is the right decision for earnings growth.”
  • Brandon Oglenski, Barclays: Questioned the Lean operating model and AI deployment. Bozeman explained the company’s approach is driven by “our people... our technology, which we think is industry leading... and then three is our Lean operating model in which that is the motor that brings all of this together.” Rajan added, “Our engineers have been at this company for a long time, and they understand this industry really well. So in terms of speed... we get incredible speed with this builder culture.”
  • Reed Seay, Stephens: Asked about headcount reductions. Bozeman stated, “We engineer the business... we're shifting to more customer focus... we're automating that. And for some of that, we're not backfilling.” Lee added, “The process itself has fundamentally changed. The technology allows us to scale at a very large magnitude.”
  • Scott Group, Wolfe Research: Queried about market share growth and personnel expense guidance. Lee clarified, “We are committed with high confidence to the $6 EPS target that we updated in our Q3 earnings call, again, with a very high confidence.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a constructive but probing tone, pressing for clarity on margin sustainability, headcount strategy, and the scalability of technology. Questions were direct, often focusing on the durability of outperformance and margin targets.
  • Management’s tone was confident and occasionally assertive, especially when explaining competitive advantages and Lean AI initiatives, as seen in Lee’s “We feel confident we'll continue to overdeliver and execute our strategy in Q1.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management sustained a similar tone—analysts remained cautious yet optimistic, while management consistently projected confidence in operational discipline, technology, and future targets.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4’s market commentary cited a continuation of challenging macro conditions, with emphasis shifting to ocean rate declines and spot trucking cost spikes. The company reported a slight decrease in combined truckload and LTL volume growth (1% vs. 3% in Q3), but highlighted ongoing market share gains.
  • Management reaffirmed double-digit productivity improvement targets and $6 EPS guidance for 2026, first introduced in Q3.
  • Strategic focus continues to center on Lean AI and automation, with no major changes in strategic direction versus Q3.
  • Analysts’ questions remained focused on margin durability, technology differentiation, and growth optionality, echoing previous quarter themes.
  • Management’s sentiment of discipline, speed, and innovation remained consistent, with a continued emphasis on scalable technology and operating leverage.

Risks and Concerns

  • Macro headwinds in global freight demand, volatile spot market costs, and declining ocean rates were cited as ongoing challenges.
  • The company acknowledged the impact of regulatory changes on trucking capacity and the unpredictability of global trade policies.
  • Lee noted restructuring charges related to workforce reductions as part of cost optimization efforts.
  • Management’s mitigation focuses on Lean AI-driven productivity, cost discipline, diversified carrier networks, and ongoing automation.

Final Takeaway

C.H. Robinson closed 2025 by demonstrating continued market share gains, disciplined cost control, and successful execution of its Lean AI transformation despite persistent macro headwinds. Management remains confident in its ability to deliver double-digit productivity gains in 2026 and maintain its $6 EPS target, supported by scalable technology, operating leverage, and a robust balance sheet. The company’s focus on automation, data-driven decision-making, and strategic flexibility positions it to outperform across market cycles while maintaining industry-leading margins and shareholder returns.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Levi Strauss projects 4%-5% organic net revenue growth for 2026 as DTC momentum accelerates
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Levi Strauss & Co. (LEVI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Michelle Gass, CEO, opened by stating the fourth quarter capped a strong year and highlighted, "In 2025, we delivered organic net revenue growth of 7%, which was broad-based across all facets of our business." She emphasized the company's focus on becoming a DTC-first, head-to-toe denim lifestyle retailer and noted, "The Levi's brand grew 7% and Levi's strengthened its standing as the #1 denim brand in the world and today, holds more market share than the next 2 global competitors combined."
  • Gass pointed to the accelerated DTC transformation, stating, "We accelerated our DTC transformation, growing 11%, which now comprises approximately half of our total business." She also cited a robust product pipeline and the completion of the global rollout of the Blue Tab premium collection.
  • On product momentum, Gass said, "In Q4, tops grew double digits, driving nearly half of our revenue growth and meaningfully higher AURs versus last year."
  • Harmit Singh, Chief Financial & Growth Officer, stated, "2025 was a strong year with continued consistent profitable growth for our company. I am pleased that our growth has accelerated over the last 3 years and we have established ourselves as a consistent mid-single-digit growth company, which we expect to continue in 2026." Singh highlighted the company's record gross margin and adjusted EBIT margin expansion, and noted a focus on mitigating tariff impacts and driving toward becoming a $10 billion DTC-first company.
  • Singh reported, "Organic net revenues grew 5% and were up 13% on a 2-year stack. Our outperformance was driven by better-than-expected results across channels and geographies." He also announced a $200 million ASR program to be completed within 3 to 6 months.

Outlook

  • Singh provided guidance for 2026, stating, "We expect organic net revenue growth of 4% to 5% with 1 point favorability from foreign exchange, resulting in reported net revenue growth of 5% to 6%." He outlined growth expectations by segment: Americas low single digits, Europe mid-single digits, and Asia mid- to high single digits.
  • Singh said, "By channel, we expect DTC to grow high single digits, fueled by positive comp sales opening 50 to 60 net new system doors and continued growth in e-commerce."
  • Gross margin is expected to be flat to prior year, with tariff impacts offset by pricing actions, higher full-price selling, product cost reduction, and SKU rationalization. Full year adjusted diluted EPS is expected to be in the range of $1.40 to $1.46.

Financial Results

  • Singh reported, "Gross margin for the quarter was 60.8% of net revenues, contracting 100 basis points relative to last year, in line with our expectations, primarily due to the impacts of tariffs, which were partially offset by pricing actions and higher full-price selling."
  • Adjusted EBIT margin contracted 180 basis points to 12.1%. Fourth quarter adjusted diluted EPS was $0.41, which includes a $0.03 headwind from a higher tax rate.
  • Inventory dollars were up 9% to prior year, "primarily driven by tariffs." The year ended with $363 million returned to shareholders, a 26% increase versus prior year.
  • Americas net revenues were up 2% for Q4. Europe net revenues accelerated, up 10%. Asia net revenues grew 4%. Beyond Yoga was up 45% in Q4 and ended the year up 17%.

Q&A

  • Laurent Vasilescu, BNP Paribas, asked about gross margins and drivers for 2026. Singh responded that "our guidance is, at this time, flat to prior year. What we have done nicely is offset the full impact of tariffs as the year progresses." He cited pricing, full-price selling, and lower product costs as mitigation factors.
  • Matthew Boss, JPMorgan, asked about the organic growth outlook and category leadership. Gass responded, "We feel really good heading into 2026...we are in the best shape that we've been in decades, both operationally and financially." Singh added that holiday sales were strong and confidence continues into Q1.
  • Jay Sole, UBS, inquired about DTC margin potential. Gass stated, "We absolutely believe that there's a lot of upside in DTC, both from a revenue standpoint and margin...margin growth will come from leverage."
  • Robert Drbul, BTIG, asked about Europe and Blue Tab performance. Singh highlighted double-digit growth in key markets and operating margin expansion. Gass said, "Blue Tab is clearly a global opportunity for us...early signs for Blue Tab are very positive."
  • Gabriella Garr, TD Cowen, asked about supply chain improvements. Gass said lead times have been reduced and SKU rationalization continues. Singh noted the U.S. distribution transition is ongoing, with benefits expected in the second half of 2026.
  • Additional analyst questions focused on pricing actions, wholesale rationalization, and SG&A leverage, with management reiterating confidence in margin expansion through operational discipline and AI-driven efficiencies.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts asked about gross margin conservatism, DTC opportunities, supply chain delays, and regional growth, showing a neutral to slightly positive but cautious tone. No significant skepticism or pressing negativity emerged; most queries centered on execution and margin drivers.
  • Management maintained a confident and upbeat tone, frequently referencing strong momentum, operational improvements, and successful transformation. Gass stated, "We are energized by the runway ahead and confident in our ability to keep driving the Levi's brand forward."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, sentiment remained positive, with a slightly greater emphasis on operational discipline, cost control, and mitigation of external headwinds.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 is more detailed, with explicit projections for organic net revenue growth (4%-5%) and EPS ($1.40 to $1.46). Previous quarter focused on raising full-year guidance for 2025 and described high single-digit growth trends.
  • Strategic priorities remain consistent: DTC acceleration, brand-led innovation, and international expansion. The current quarter places greater focus on margin expansion through AI-driven efficiencies and cost discipline.
  • Management confidence is strong in both quarters, though the current quarter underscores progress in managing tariffs and operational transitions. Analyst tone is steady, seeking more granularity on execution and mitigation strategies.
  • Key metrics such as gross margin, EBIT margin, and DTC growth are compared with prior results, highlighting the company's ability to sustain growth amid external challenges.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management pointed to ongoing tariff impacts, mitigated through pricing, cost reduction, and rationalization. Singh noted, "We are making meaningful progress on mitigating tariff impacts on our P&L through targeted pricing actions...and prudent management of our cost base."
  • The U.S. distribution transition is taking longer than expected, resulting in higher transitional costs, but management expects completion in 2026.
  • Analysts raised questions on wholesale rationalization, SG&A leverage, and supply chain lead times, with management providing detailed mitigation strategies and expressing confidence in execution.

Final Takeaway

Levi Strauss & Co. closed 2025 with broad-based revenue growth, strengthened DTC performance, and expanding product categories. Management projects continued mid-single-digit organic growth and flat gross margins for 2026, with targeted actions to offset tariffs and drive margin expansion. The company’s confidence is underscored by strong brand positioning, operational improvements, and a robust product pipeline, positioning Levi’s for ongoing growth and shareholder value creation in the year ahead.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Amphenol outlines $6.9B–$7B Q1 2026 sales target as CCS acquisition expands AI exposure
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Amphenol Corporation (APH) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO R. Norwitt highlighted a record-breaking fourth quarter, citing "sales and adjusted diluted earnings per share in the fourth quarter, both exceeding the high end of our guidance." He emphasized robust growth across nearly all end markets and pointed to a record $8.4 billion in orders, noting, "These robust orders were driven primarily by data center demand related in particular to artificial intelligence investments being planned by a number of our large customers."
  • Norwitt announced the closure of the Trexon acquisition, which expands Amphenol's defense market offerings, and confirmed the earlier-than-expected closing of the CommScope (CCS) acquisition, stating, "We expect CommScope to generate full year 2026 sales of $4.1 billion and to add $0.15 to Amphenol's 2026 adjusted earnings per share."
  • CFO Craig Lampo reported, "The company closed the fourth quarter of 2025 with record sales of $6.4 billion and GAAP and adjusted diluted EPS of $0.97 and $0.93, respectively." Lampo also highlighted a "very strong book-to-bill ratio of 1.31:1," driven by IT datacom market bookings, and discussed a robust liquidity position and the impact of the CCS acquisition on future interest expenses.

Outlook

  • Management guided Q1 2026 sales in the range of $6.9 billion to $7 billion and adjusted diluted EPS of $0.91 to $0.93. Norwitt stated, "This would represent significant sales growth from prior year of 43% to 45% and adjusted diluted EPS growth of 44% to 48%."
  • Guidance includes approximately $900 million in sales and $0.02 of adjusted EPS accretion from the CommScope acquisition.
  • Lampo indicated the adjusted tax rate of 25.5% will continue into 2026.

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter sales were $6.4 billion, up 49% in U.S. dollars and 37% organically year-over-year, with sequential sales up 4%.
  • Adjusted diluted EPS reached $0.97, up 76% from the prior year. GAAP operating margin was 26.8%, with adjusted operating margin at 27.5%.
  • Record orders of $8.431 billion for the quarter led to a book-to-bill ratio of 1.31:1, primarily driven by IT datacom demand for AI applications.
  • Full-year sales were $23.1 billion, up 52%, and adjusted operating margin was 26.2%.
  • Segment performance: Communications Solutions sales grew 78%, Harsh Environment Solutions up 31%, Interconnect and Sensor Systems up 21% (all year-over-year).
  • Operating cash flow for the quarter was $1.7 billion, with free cash flow at $1.5 billion.
  • The company repurchased 1.3 million shares and returned $373 million to shareholders during the quarter.

Q&A

  • William Stein, Truist Securities: Asked about the nature and concentration of strong bookings and order duration. Norwitt explained, "These robust orders were driven primarily by data center demand related in particular to artificial intelligence investments... this is not because of kind of getting in line, so to speak... it's giving us the comfort through their own commitments to Amphenol that we should then make the commitments in capital."
  • Amit Daryanani, Evercore ISI: Asked about the breadth of offerings post-CCS deal and fiber optics exposure. Norwitt stated, "With CCS... CCS vaults us into a position of breadth and depth in the technology around fiber optic interconnect that is a real expansion of our capabilities."
  • Luke Junk, Baird: Inquired on integration steps for CommScope. Norwitt responded, "That word integration is not a word in the Amphenol lexicon... what we do talk about is letting them evolve into the Amphenol family, letting them be who they were because it's a fabulous organization."
  • Wamsi Mohan, BofA Securities: Asked about Q1 IT datacom organic outlook and AI program growth. Norwitt described broad-based customer engagement, indicating, "We have an enormous position with a lot of different customers up and down the stack of AI... their investment plans are all going up."
  • Additional analyst questions focused on order sustainability, margin outlook, CapEx trajectory, and specific end-market trends, with management emphasizing confidence in operational culture and future growth.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed strong interest in AI-driven growth, order momentum, and the impact of acquisitions, with questions probing sustainability, competitive positioning, and capital allocation. Tone was positive but probing for clarity on long-term order visibility and integration strategy.
  • Management maintained a confident and upbeat tone in prepared remarks, frequently citing "record" achievements and emphasizing operational agility. In Q&A, Norwitt and Lampo provided detailed explanations, often expressing pride and confidence, while occasionally clarifying on the nuances of integration and investment.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management displayed increased enthusiasm, with a greater focus on AI, acquisition benefits, and operational scale.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for Q1 2026 sales ($6.9B–$7B) and EPS ($0.91–$0.93) represents a notable step up from Q4 2025 guidance. The CCS acquisition was closed earlier than anticipated and is expected to significantly impact sales and EPS.
  • Book-to-bill ratio surged to 1.31:1 (from 0.99:1 in Q3), reflecting record orders, especially in IT datacom/AI.
  • Segment growth rates accelerated in Communications Solutions and Harsh Environment Solutions compared to Q3.
  • Management tone grew more bullish, highlighting large-scale acquisition integration and robust order momentum.
  • Analysts' questions shifted toward order sustainability, future CapEx, and acquisition synergies versus the prior quarter's focus on margin incrementals and segment profitability.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged a $100 million discrete tax accrual related to notices from Chinese tax authorities, with ongoing discussions on appropriate tax positions.
  • Higher adjusted tax rate (25.5%) due to income mix shift to higher-tax jurisdictions is expected to persist.
  • Potential margin headwinds from the integration of CCS, metals costs, and seasonality were discussed, but Lampo stated, "I wouldn't say that, that, at least as of now, we see having any significant impact on kind of our margin outlook as we're moving into '26."
  • Supply chain constraints, metals prices, and global geopolitical volatility were noted as ongoing management challenges, mitigated by the company's decentralized operating culture.

Final Takeaway

Amphenol closed 2025 with record sales and earnings, driven by broad-based demand and outstanding operational execution, especially in AI-related markets. The early completion of the CommScope acquisition significantly expands Amphenol's fiber optic and industrial portfolio, positioning the company for further growth in 2026. Management projects robust sales and EPS for Q1 2026, buoyed by record orders, a strong book-to-bill ratio, and continued momentum in key end markets, while remaining vigilant around tax, integration, and supply chain risks.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Raymond James signals continued adviser recruiting growth and targets $400M share repurchases amid robust loan expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Raymond James Financial (RJF) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Paul Shoukry highlighted "strong results this quarter" driven by Raymond James' "client-first culture," technology investments, and a "robust technology and products platform," resulting in an 8% annualized net new asset growth. Shoukry reported adviser recruiting momentum, with $96 million in trailing 12-month production for new advisers and approximately $13 billion of client assets at previous firms, noting, "a strong result for a quarter that typically experiences a seasonal slowdown." Over the past year, newly recruited assets reached more than $69 billion across all platforms.

  • Management emphasized ongoing investments, including the launch of a proprietary digital AI operations agent named Rai, as part of a "service-focused long-term AI strategy." Shoukry described the recent acquisition of Clark Capital Management, with over $46 billion in combined discretionary and nondiscretionary assets, as a move to "further strengthen Raymond James Investment Management's existing investment and wealth planning offerings."

  • Shoukry also referenced continued capital deployment: "This quarter, we repurchased $400 million of common stock at an average share price of $162. We ended the quarter with a Tier 1 leverage ratio of 12.7%."

  • CFO Jonathan Oorlog stated, "The firm reported record net revenues of $3.7 billion for the fiscal first quarter. Net income available to common shareholders was $562 million with earnings per diluted share of $2.79. Excluding expenses related to acquisitions, adjusted net income available to common shareholders equaled $577 million, resulting in adjusted earnings per diluted share of $2.86."

Outlook

  • Management expects fiscal second quarter 2026 asset management and related administrative fees to be "higher by approximately 1% over the first quarter level," citing the impact of two fewer billing days. The company projects noncompensation expenses for the fiscal year (excluding certain provisions and adjustments) to be about $2.3 billion, representing approximately 8% growth over the prior year.

  • Shoukry reiterated a focus on "organic growth as the #1 capital priority," with continued investment in technology and adviser recruiting expected to remain central. Oorlog confirmed a target of approximately $400 million in common share repurchases for the fiscal second quarter.

Financial Results

  • Raymond James reported record net revenues of $3.7 billion. Net income available to common shareholders was $562 million, and earnings per diluted share reached $2.79. Adjusted earnings per diluted share were $2.86, and the adjusted pretax margin was 20%.

  • Private Client Group produced record net revenues of $2.77 billion and pretax income of $439 million. Asset Management segment achieved record pretax income of $143 million on record net revenues of $326 million. The Bank segment reported net revenues of $487 million and record pretax income of $173 million, with loans ending the quarter at $53.4 billion.

  • Asset management and related administrative fees approached $2 billion, up 15% over the prior year and 6% over the preceding quarter. PCG fee-based assets reached $1.04 trillion at quarter end, up 19% year-over-year. Securities-based lending balances grew 28% annually and 10% in the quarter.

Q&A

  • Y. Cho, JPMorgan: Asked about the sustainability and drivers of net new asset growth. Shoukry responded, "$31 billion of net new assets in the quarter would be our second best quarter ever...the recruiting activity is robust...the retention of our existing advisers remains very strong...having a platform where advisers feel like there's a culture that really respects the independents and their book ownership."

  • Benjamin Budish, Barclays: Inquired about competitive intensity in adviser recruiting. Shoukry explained, "The environment though is competitive...the advisers we're recruiting are not looking for a 3- to 5-year destination. They're looking for a much longer...platform and a home for them, their teams and their clients where they're not going to have to have another disruption in 3 to 5 years."

  • Craig Siegenthaler, BofA: Asked about sustainability of 8% net new asset growth. Shoukry stated, "8% did benefit from not only the really strong retention results, recruiting results, but also there's year-end...dynamics...we're confident that...we can continue to be a leading grower in wealth management."

  • William Katz, TD Cowen: Sought clarity on the Clark Capital transaction and capital deployment. Shoukry called Clark Capital "a perfect representation of our M&A priorities" and confirmed ongoing share repurchase targets.

  • Steven Chubak, Wolfe Research: Compared M&A advisory results to peers. Shoukry noted "public company M&A...definitely led the way in the recovery...we feel very confident with our expertise...with the sectors that we are very good in, in our pipelines."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was generally positive but included probing on sustainability of adviser growth, competitive pressures, and capital deployment, with some skepticism about replicability of high net new asset growth.

  • Management maintained a confident tone throughout, emphasizing long-term strategy and resilience against industry competition. Shoukry used phrases such as "we are confident" and "we're optimistic" repeatedly, while also acknowledging short-term competitive challenges.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts were slightly more focused on competition and adviser movement, but management's tone stayed consistently confident.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Net new asset annualized growth accelerated from 5% in Q4 2025 to 8% in Q1 2026.

  • Adviser recruiting and client asset growth increased, with new assets recruited over the last 12 months rising from nearly $63 billion to more than $69 billion.

  • Securities-based lending growth stepped up from 22% annual in Q4 2025 to 28% in Q1 2026.

  • Capital Markets segment revenues declined sequentially, after a strong Q4, as management flagged lower M&A and advisory revenues in Q1 2026.

  • Management's tone remained confident, but with slightly more attention given to competition and the sustainability of recent growth rates compared to prior calls.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted increased competition for adviser recruiting, particularly from private equity-backed roll-ups, but stressed their differentiation through culture and technology investment.

  • Interest rate declines were cited as a headwind for certain revenue streams, impacting noncompensable revenues and deposit mix.

  • Management warned of the unpredictability in closing M&A transactions and the impact of fewer billing and earning days in the upcoming quarter.

  • Analysts raised concerns about margin sustainability, compensation ratios, and the impact of competition on adviser retention and recruiting costs.

Final Takeaway

Raymond James Financial began fiscal 2026 with record net revenues, strong adviser recruiting, and significant growth in assets and loans, especially in securities-based lending. The firm remains committed to technology investment, organic growth, and targeted acquisitions, while maintaining disciplined capital deployment through consistent share repurchases. Management expressed confidence in sustaining growth amid competitive pressures, highlighting a robust recruiting pipeline and continued focus on adviser and client relationships as key differentiators for the future.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: ServiceNow projects 20% subscription revenue growth for 2026 with expanded AI platform and $5B share repurchase
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ServiceNow (NOW) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO William McDermott stated that "Q4 results beat expectations handily, just like we have consistently for years now." He emphasized that subscription revenue growth was 21%, which was "1.5 points above the high end of our guidance." McDermott highlighted the acceleration in net new annual contract value (NNACV) growth, a 25% increase in monthly active users, and significant expansion in AI-driven products such as Now Assist and the AI Control Tower. He announced, "we're guiding to 20% subscription revenue growth for 2026" and described ServiceNow as "the gateway" to enterprise AI adoption, underscoring a long-term platform strategy that positions the company as "the AI defining enterprise software company in the 21st century."

  • McDermott also detailed the acquisitions of Veza and Armis, stating, "This assembles 3 critical layers for enterprises to operate securely in agentic AI world, visibility, identity and orchestration." He explained that no further large-scale M&A is anticipated, and the company's organic growth strategy "remains unchanged."

  • ServiceNow announced "an incremental USD 5 billion share repurchase authorization with an immediate ASR of $2 billion."

  • President & CFO Gina Mastantuono reported, "Q4 subscription revenues were $3.466 billion, growing 19.5% year-over-year in constant currency, exceeding the high end of our guidance range by 150 basis points." She highlighted a "robust 98% renewal rate in Q4," increases in large deals, and a "free cash flow margin was 57%, up 950 basis points year-over-year." Mastantuono attributed margin strength to "OpEx efficiencies and disciplined spend management." She also noted, "We ended 2025 with a healthy balance sheet of over $10 billion in cash and investments."

Outlook

  • Mastantuono provided 2026 guidance: "We expect subscription revenues between $15.53 billion and $15.57 billion, representing 19.5% to 20% year-over-year growth on a constant currency basis." She forecasted a subscription gross margin of 82%, operating margin of 32%, and free cash flow margin of 36%. For Q1, she projected subscription revenues between $3.650 billion and $3.655 billion, with current RPO growth of 20% on a constant currency basis and operating margin of 31.5%.

Financial Results

  • Q4 subscription revenues reached $3.466 billion, up 19.5% year-over-year in constant currency. Remaining performance obligations (RPO) stood at approximately $28.2 billion, a 22.5% year-over-year increase. Current RPO was $12.85 billion, up 21% year-over-year. Free cash flow for 2025 totaled $4.6 billion, with a free cash flow margin of 35%. ServiceNow repurchased approximately 3.6 million shares in Q4.

  • Product highlights included Now Assist ACV surpassing $600 million, 244 deals greater than $1 million in NNACV, and significant growth in Workflow Data Fabric and AI Control Tower deployments. The company reported "Moveworks contributed 1 point to both RPO and cRPO."

Q&A

  • Aleksandr Zukin, Wolfe Research: Questioned AI monetization and demand tailwinds. McDermott responded that "our user count is growing. You can see it's growing in harmony with our revenue. And you can also see that our margins are growing." Amit Zavery added that "the consumption part has been adding to our subscription revenue quickly as well."

  • Sanjit Singh, Morgan Stanley: Inquired about federal business performance. McDermott explained, "What didn't happen in 2025 is only good news for 2026. ... the global government business is on fire across Europe, Middle East and obviously, Asia."

  • Gabriela Borges, Goldman Sachs: Asked about gross margin headwinds. Mastantuono stated, "the bulk of it is actually our very strategic focus on moving more towards hyperscalers that have slightly lower gross margins at this stage of the game," but said operating margin and free cash flow guidance were increased due to efficiencies.

  • Samad Samana, Jefferies: Asked about future M&A and Armis financials. McDermott replied, "We do not have a large-scale M&A on the road map." Mastantuono projected Armis would contribute about 1 point to subscription revenue in '26 and up to a 50 bps headwind to operating margin.

  • Peter Weed, Bernstein: Queried about OpenAI and Anthropic partnerships. Zavery explained, "we allow customer choice... There might be some unique use cases, which will be done with individual providers like OpenAI and Anthropic."

  • Patrick Walravens, JMP Securities: Raised concerns about AI agent governance. Zavery responded, "the reason we launched AI Control Tower early last year... is because we're addressing these things head on... That allows you to do kill switches, where you can now go and shut down any agent, which is going rogue."

  • Matthew Hedberg, RBC: Asked about pricing models. Zavery stated, "the hybrid model has seemed to be resonating with my customers. They know what the envelope they have, what they will be consuming beyond that, how much will it cost them?"

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was positive, with multiple congratulations and recognition of strong results and durability. Analysts pressed for details on AI monetization, federal growth, and M&A but showed optimism on ServiceNow's positioning.

  • Management maintained a confident and assertive tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, with McDermott and Mastantuono emphasizing outperformance, strategic clarity, and an optimistic outlook. Statements such as "we're guiding to 20% subscription revenue growth for 2026" and "our confidence in sustained high organic growth has never been greater" reflect elevated confidence compared to prior quarters.

  • Compared to Q3, management's tone was even more self-assured, focusing on transformational AI opportunities and platform expansion.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 was introduced at 19.5% to 20% subscription revenue growth, compared to raised 2025 guidance in Q3. The new $5 billion share repurchase and $2 billion ASR were not present in the previous quarter. Strategic acquisitions (Veza, Armis) and expansion in AI platform capabilities were more prominent.

  • Management's tone in Q4 was more assertive, forecasting higher operational leverage and highlighting the company's status as a platform leader. Analysts in Q4 focused more on monetization of AI, federal pipeline, and partnership strategy, while Q3 included more questions about implementation and product breadth.

  • Large-deal volume, AI product ACV, and free cash flow margins all showed notable increases versus Q3.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted increased gross margin headwinds from a "strategic focus on moving more towards hyperscalers," but offset these with operating efficiencies.

  • Security risk was cited due to expanded AI adoption, with McDermott describing AI as "expanding the attack surface exponentially." The integration of Veza and Armis is presented as the core mitigation.

  • Analysts raised questions about federal deal timing, potential dilution from acquisitions, and the complexity of hybrid pricing models.

Final Takeaway

ServiceNow management emphasized a record-breaking Q4 and full-year 2025, driven by platform expansion, strong AI monetization, and significant large-deal momentum. The company projects continued high growth in 2026, underpinned by an expanded AI platform, new security capabilities, disciplined capital allocation, and a major share repurchase initiative. Management's commentary reflects strong confidence in both operational performance and strategic positioning for the next phase of enterprise AI adoption.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Microsoft outlines $80.65B–$81.75B Q3 revenue target amid accelerating AI cloud demand and Copilot seat growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) Q2 2026

Management View

  • CEO Satya Nadella highlighted that "the Microsoft Cloud surpassed $50 billion in revenue for the first time, up 26% year-over-year, reflecting the strength of our platform and accelerating demand." Nadella described the company as being in "the beginning phases of AI diffusion and its broad GDP impact," with a rapidly expanding total addressable market across the technology stack.
  • Nadella detailed major infrastructure developments, including "a 50% increase in throughput... in one of our highest volume workloads, OpenAI inferencing, powering our Copilots," and the launch of the Maia 200 accelerator, which "delivers 10-plus petaFLOPS at FP4 precision with over 30% improved TCO compared to the latest generation hardware in our fleet." The company added "nearly 1 gigawatt of total capacity this quarter alone."
  • Microsoft expanded its AI model ecosystem, adding "support for GPT-5.2 as well as Claude 4.5," and noted over 1,500 customers using both Anthropic and OpenAI models on Foundry. Fabric's revenue run rate is "now over $2 billion with over 31,000 customers," up 60% year-over-year. Foundry customers spending $1 million plus per quarter grew nearly 80%, and over 250 customers are on track to process over 1 trillion tokens this year.
  • Copilot momentum was emphasized: "Daily users of our Copilot app increased nearly 3x year-over-year." Microsoft 365 Copilot seat adds were up over 160% year-over-year, reaching 15 million paid seats, and the number of customers with over 35,000 seats tripled year-over-year.
  • CFO Amy Hood stated, "With growing demand for our offerings and focused execution by our sales teams, we again exceeded expectations across revenue, operating income and earnings per share, while investing to fuel long-term growth." Hood added, "This quarter, revenue was $81.3 billion, up 17%... Earnings per share was $4.14, an increase of 24%."

Outlook

  • Microsoft expects Q3 total company revenue of $80.65 billion to $81.75 billion or growth of 15% to 17%. COGS is projected at $26.65 billion to $26.85 billion with growth of 22% to 23%. Operating expense is anticipated at $17.8 billion to $17.9 billion, up 10% to 11%.
  • Operating margins are expected to be down slightly year-over-year, excluding OpenAI investments. Microsoft Cloud gross margin percentage is projected to be roughly 65%, down year-over-year due to continued investments in AI.
  • For Productivity and Business Processes, revenue guidance is $34.25 billion to $34.55 billion (14% to 15% growth), with M365 Commercial Cloud revenue growth of 13% to 14% in constant currency. Dynamics 365 is expected to grow revenue in the high teens. Intelligent Cloud revenue is forecast at $34.1 billion to $34.4 billion (27% to 29% growth), with Azure revenue growth between 37% and 38% in constant currency. More Personal Computing revenue is expected between $12.3 billion and $12.8 billion.

Financial Results

  • Microsoft reported revenue of $81.3 billion, up 17%. Gross margin dollars increased 16%, and operating income rose 21%. Earnings per share was $4.14, an increase of 24%.
  • Capital expenditures reached $37.5 billion, with roughly two-thirds allocated to short-lived assets (primarily GPUs and CPUs). Cash flow from operations was $35.8 billion, and free cash flow was $5.9 billion. The company returned $12.7 billion to shareholders through dividends and share repurchases, an increase of 32% year-over-year.
  • Commercial bookings increased 230%, driven by large Azure commitments from OpenAI and Anthropic. Remaining performance obligation (RPO) grew to $625 billion, with 45% from OpenAI.
  • Microsoft Cloud revenue was $51.5 billion, up 26%. Productivity and Business Processes revenue was $34.1 billion, Intelligent Cloud revenue was $32.9 billion, and More Personal Computing revenue was $14.3 billion.

Q&A

  • Keith Weiss, Morgan Stanley: Questioned the ROI on CapEx spending and Azure growth. Amy Hood responded that CapEx is allocated across Azure, M365 Copilot, GitHub Copilot, and R&D, stating "this is about investing in all the layers of the stack that benefit customers." Satya Nadella added, "we don't want to maximize just one business... we want to be able to allocate capacity while we're sort of supply constrained in a way that allows us to essentially build the best LTV portfolio."
  • Mark Moerdler, Bernstein: Asked about the ability to capture sufficient revenue over the hardware's 6-year life, given RPO duration. Hood stated, "the majority of the capital that we're spending today... are already contracted for most of their useful life."
  • Brent Thill, Jefferies: Inquired about the durability of the 45% backlog related to OpenAI. Hood responded, "55% or roughly $350 billion is related to the breadth of our portfolio... And when you think about that portion alone growing 28%, it's really impressive work."
  • Karl Keirstead, UBS: Sought comments on the magnitude of capacity adds and global expansion. Hood stated, "We're working as hard as we can to add capacity as quickly as we can... A lot of that will be added in the United States... but it also needs to be added across the globe to meet the customer demand."
  • Mark Murphy, JPMorgan: Asked about Maia 200's significance in silicon and margin support. Nadella said, "We're very, very thrilled about the progress with Maia 200... It's just not even about just the silicon, the way the networking works at rack scale... we are not locked into any one thing."
  • Brad Zelnick, Deutsche Bank: Queried AI stack adoption momentum. Nadella explained, "the most important database underneath for any company that uses Microsoft today is the data underneath Microsoft 365... the agent black plane is really transforming companies."
  • Raimo Lenschow, Barclays: Asked about CPU and cloud transitions. Nadella clarified, "AI workloads are not just AI accelerator compute... we think about our commercial cloud and keep it balanced with the rest of our AI Cloud because when clients bring their workloads and build new workloads, they need all of these infrastructure elements."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed concerns about CapEx growth, ROI, and backlog concentration, with a slightly negative tone on the sustainability of investments and customer diversity.
  • Management remained confident but occasionally defensive, using phrases like "we feel great about it" and "we are using all of that, obviously, to optimize for the long term," while emphasizing breadth and long-term contracts.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone stayed confident but increased focus on explaining CapEx allocation and customer contract durability, while analysts showed heightened concern about concentration and investment returns.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue rose to $81.3 billion from $77.7 billion previously, while operating income, gross margin, and EPS also increased. Copilot seat growth accelerated, and Microsoft Cloud revenue exceeded $50 billion for the first time.
  • Commercial bookings jumped further, and RPO increased to $625 billion from nearly $400 billion, with a larger portion attributed to OpenAI. CapEx grew to $37.5 billion from $34.9 billion, with continued emphasis on short-lived assets.
  • Management's focus on the breadth of product offerings and long-term contract security intensified, and analysts directed more questions to CapEx ROI and concentration risk compared to the prior quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing supply constraints in cloud infrastructure, increased CapEx, and the need to balance investments across business lines.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the ROI of CapEx and concentration in OpenAI-related commitments. Hood explained that "much of that risk... isn't there, because they're already sold for the entirety of their useful life."
  • Other risks include potential volatility in memory pricing, capacity delivery timing, and the impact on gross margins from sustained investments in AI infrastructure.

Final Takeaway

Microsoft delivered record financial results in Q2 2026, surpassing $50 billion in Microsoft Cloud revenue and achieving substantial growth in Copilot adoption and AI infrastructure. The company highlighted a strategic focus on AI, broadening its platform ecosystem, and securing long-term contracts that support elevated CapEx investments. Management expressed confidence in continued momentum across commercial and enterprise lines, supported by global capacity expansion and diversified revenue streams, while acknowledging the need to address analyst concerns about investment returns and customer concentration moving forward.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
